I think some things are not the right naming convention, and I'm worried that calling these things "global files" again just invites confusion. But it looks like it ought to work.

I think we should avoid autogenerating code because it adds a lot of extra complexity. Inheritance sounds an idea worth investigating.

Also perhaps if we can make all kernels to follow the same pattern:
```
@MY_METHOD.implements(features.Image, pil_kernel=_F.MY_METHOD)
def _MY_METHOD_image(input: features.Image, *, ...) -> features.Image:
    output = F.MY_METHOD(input, ...)

    return features.Image.new_like(input, output)
```

Then we could eliminate also their method bodies:

```
@MY_METHOD.implements(features.Image, pil_kernel=_F.MY_METHOD, tensor_kernel= F.MY_METHOD)
def _MY_METHOD_image(input: features.Image, *, ...) -> features.Image: pass
```

If that's true, then perhaps we don't even need those intermediate per type kernels and all we need is to define these directly as arguments to the `@Dispatcher` annotation. I don't know if that's possible, let me know your thoughts.

@jreiberkyle my only concern is about extra variable assignments being mistaken by future readers as being significant instead of being only incidental. So maybe try to avoid them as much as possible and document them where they are used? Just a suggestion, not a requirement.

There are a couple changes in the PR that I have suggested. These are optional and do not affect the functionality of the code. Overall, I approve the merging of this pull request ✅ .

But, I am also adding the following so we know which direction to go for future improvements.

### Error handling: still not ideal

There is a still one thing about our error handling that bothers me. I can illustrate it with the following error message which was generated by raising a plain exception in the function body:

```
# >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>> ERROR REPORT <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1787, in parse_known_args
        namespace, args = self._parse_known_args(args, namespace)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1996, in _parse_known_args
        stop_index = consume_positionals(start_index)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1952, in consume_positionals
        take_action(action, args)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1845, in take_action
        argument_values = self._get_values(action, argument_strings)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 2386, in _get_values
        self._check_value(action, value[0])
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 2433, in _check_value
        raise ArgumentError(action, msg % args)
    argparse.ArgumentError: argument command: invalid choice: 'string-art' (choose from 'clean', 'compare', 'config', 'create', 'info', 'init', 'install', 'list', 'package', 'remove', 'rename', 'run', 'search', 'uninstall', 'update', 'upgrade', 'notices')

    During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/conda/exceptions.py", line 1133, in __call__
        return func(*args, **kwargs)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/conda/cli/main.py", line 63, in main_subshell
        args = p.parse_args(args)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1755, in parse_args
        args, argv = self.parse_known_args(args, namespace)
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/devenv/Darwin/envs/devenv-3.7-c/lib/python3.7/argparse.py", line 1794, in parse_known_args
        self.error(str(err))
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/conda/conda/cli/conda_argparse.py", line 197, in error
        sys.exit(subcommand.action(sys.argv[2:]))
      File "/Users/travishathaway/dev/string-art/string_art.py", line 26, in sub_command
        raise Exception("Hai")
    Exception: Hai
```

We're now bubbling up an exception, which is good, but because of the way that we use `argparse` we also get a weird looking exception from that code as well. This admittedly isn't that bad, but it could really confuse developers working on new plugins. They may choose to resolve the first error, only to later discover that this exception is in fact part of the functioning code.

### Potential improvements

During the first week of August as I was playing around with the new plugins code, I really wanted to tackle this problem myself and actually wrote up an alternative implementation that addresses these issues:

https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/11680

This implementation also enables support for CLI argument parsing libraries like `click` (see the example in the PR).

For future iterations, we should try to integrate some of the changes I suggested there.

The only other high level feedback I have is that introducing an interface type `Value` will lose some type-safety.
The way this is written, the types `BoolValue` and `StringValue` present the type of the underlying value (`bool` or `string`). Once those instances are bound to a local of type `Value` (an interface type), you'll have to ascertain the type of the underlying value using the `type()` method to error-check you've got a flag of the right type. Checking the `type()` method is going to introduce some boilerplate and requires a runtime check. If you eliminate the `Variable` interface entirely, then users are forced to work with `BoolValue` and `StringValue` directly, which requires them to know how they plan to use the flag ahead of time.

If you're using the `interface` type to reduce library boilerplate, then you might be able to use a `Variable` type with a generic argument for `Bool` and `String`.

Thanks for this Yarik, looks like a great start. I have a few opinions/questions/requests...

1) It would be good to document what happens if there is an exception cancelling a task midway through. I'm not sure if that can happen, since I guess it happens in a single DB transaction, so the whole transaction would fail if there was an issue updating one task - is that correct? Is there anything we need to document here about possible partial success cases?
2) I'm not 100% sure we should operate directly on the database. Perhaps there are significant performance improvements with direct DB access, but I'm worried that the two APIs to cancel tasks may diverge in their implementation or introduce subtle differences, especially in regards to how they handle scopes.
3) I tend to think this API call would be better if it _did_ prevent future tasks from being added to the task group. Since a decision task can run for up to 17 minutes after being cancelled, it seems reasonable to me that we should be able to prevent the Queue from allowing tasks to be created with a given taskGroupId, so that that decision task would be ineffective. Without this, it seems quite easy/possible/likely that cancelTaskGroup will often be ineffective and cause frustration since a graph could be cancelled, and then 15 minutes later spawn a large number of tasks.

Although it doesn't currently exist, maybe we should expose it as an API call directly (e.g. `queue.FreezeTaskGroup`), which `queue.cancelTaskGroup` could call under the hood. But e.g. the decision task could also call it in taskgraph, after it finishes adding all tasks, as some offer of safety that the task graph isn't later modified.

Maybe we can discuss this with the broader community in the Taskcluster Community Meeting later today.

One question I have is around our `IsVirtualEnv` and `VirtualEnvCommand` functions.

- `IsVirtualEnv` checks if there's a `python.exe` binary inside the specified virtual environment directory, via call to `os.Stat` looking for `Scripts/python.exe`
- `VirtualEnvCommand` returns an `exec.Cmd` for the binary in `Scripts/<bin>.exe`. This function is used to run `Scripts/python.exe` inside the virtual environment.

Do these work on systems where Python is installed from the app store? (Basically, when we run `pulumi-python-shim.cmd <resolved_execution_alias_python_binary> -m venv venv` to create the virtual environment, in the resulting `venv` directory, what type of file is `venv/Scripts/python.exe`? Can we successfully `os.Stat` and `exec.Cmd` it, or is it one of these execution aliases that needs `Lstat` and shimming?)

A way to test it: With the changes in this PR, create a standard Python Pulumi project via `pulumi new python` and then run `pulumi up`. That should exercise these code paths.

Thanks for taking care of this!

> I chose to use the PyPi URL for the download of the dependency as I had difficulties finding the sha256 hashes for the version of the library at mirror.tensorflow.org and github.com (they differ from the PyPi one).

It's fine to just generate the sha256 hash locally - i.e. download the tarball and compute the hash yourself.  In this case it might be easier to still do that, since the github one is already mirrored (see other comment).

FWIW, to justify that a little - the point of the hash isn't really to verify the integrity of the file that you're downloading today from the server, because in general we assume you're downloading it via HTTPS, on a reasonably secure machine and network, etc, and it doesn't really add much protection to separately get a hash from the same server rather than just computing it yourself.  The point is to ensure the server doesn't change the contents of the file in the future.  That way, we reduce the amount of trust we need to have from "I trust this server in perpetuity" to "I trust this server right now, today".

I agree that we need a spec here. I think we're going to have to work together to come up with one. I'm pretty sure that we do eventually to port the cycle-breaking code that @t0yv0 added last year to the other SDKs. However, I think that what you have here works, and we should probably port it to the other SDKs as well.

If I had to give an _informal_ spec of the expected behavior as of today, I'd say that it's something like this. The dependencies of a resource `R` is the set of custom and remote component resources reachable through the union of `R`'s implicit and explicit dependencies (i.e. resources referred to by `R`'s inputs and those present in `R`'s `dependsOn` option, if any. A resource `A` is _reachable_ from another resource `B` if `B` is a non-remote component resource and `A` is one of its descendants.

I think that the changes in this PR agree with that informal statement.

The idea of having `config/crypto.h` and define the size of the contexts for different crypto operations sounds reasonable to me, but the downside is that we need to manage the numbers manually (instead of having something like `sizeof(mbedtls_aes_context)` where compiler determines the proper size).

One idea/suggestion on this: 
- We can consider adding a new config `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CRYPTO_LIBRARY` to indicate to OT core which crypto library is being used, 
   - e.g., we can have `CRYPTO_LIB_MBEDTLS_Vxxx` or `CRYPTO_LIB_PSA` or `CRYPTO_LIB_OTHER` (for vendor/platform specific ones) etc). 
- Then based this config we can include the proper headers and then use `sizeof(mbedtls_aes_context)` for the context constants. 
- Or maybe even directly use `mbedtls_aes_context` as the definition of context depending on the `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CRYPTO_LIBRARY` in the core implementation (e.g., in `Crypto::AesEcb` class). 

Thoughts?

- If the kernel is new while the frontend is old, the kernel will keep the same behavior for "update" messages. As long as the frontend successfully discards the unknown "echo_update" message without failing (and our code does this), then everything will be as expected.
- If the kernel is old, while the frontend is new, the frontend will build up a finitely sized collections of attribute keys to message ids that it is looking for, but no performance hit or change in behavior.

As such, the main point is then about correct behavior, which is compatible with the RC phase. I do have some points of improvements though:

- Ideally in this PR, but can be done in follow up in RC phase: We should add one or more tests for the behavior when we set the env var to use / not use echos. Currently this is not covered by the tests?
- Follow up PR: We should add tests for the JS side of echos. Currently there are no tests checking this, and I think I could simplify the JS code for echo handling some more, but it is hard to be certain since non of the behavior is encoded in tests.
- Follow up PR: Can we add a note in the docstring of `send_sync_message` about what the expected return value is? E.g. "If a message is sent successfully, this returns the message ID of that message. Otherwise it returns an empty string".

> @bmw I have second thoughts about the naming pattern `Generic<ClassName>` for the public `TypeVar` instances introduced in the project. Given that the `TypeVar` itself is not a generic, but the variable of a generic representing "any" possible type of a given bound, I think a better name would be `Any<ClassName>` (eg. `AnyJSONObjectWithField`, `AnyImmutableMap`).
>
> But if is too late, I can live with `Generic<ClassName>` and prefer to keep a coherent convention for `GenericTypedJSONObjectWithFields`.

Sorry I missed this question earlier. I personally don't love the `Any*` name. In this PR, the return value for `register` isn't **any** `TypedJSONObjectWithFields` and is instead the specific subclass passed in as the parameter. Maybe `TypedJSONObjectWithFieldsGeneric`, `TypedJSONObjectWithFieldsType`, or `TypedJSONObjectWithFieldsTypeVar` is better?

I personally am in favor of just sticking with our current convention though. It's not perfect, but I think the name "Generic" helps communicate it is a Python typing generic and including the class name helps communicate the bounds of the type.

This is another great effort, but I think it still has a bit to go.  Thanks for taking another crack.  I think you probably would've caught most of these things if you checked against the compare.json file.  

It's not obvious and maybe it should be, but when you run the tests for the first time it generates a JSON file to compare your HTML against.  If the parser doesn't parse correctly it generates a bad JSON file to test against.  

I think that's what happened here.  You ran the tests and it generated bad data, so all the subsequent tests did not fail.  Check the JSON file against the HTML to make sure it's working.  The tests really are to make sure the overall juriscraper structure as much (and maybe more) than individual courts.
